feedback.text: My comments concern: Dark patterns in gaming/gambling mechanisms, in relation to my country’s laws. Information trading/attention economy; Company Hyres applicant/transfer to rent products instead of owning their purchases. The laws of non-EU countries have an impact on my online consumption. The largest beet suppliers use a kind of oligopoly to control consumption. In Sweden, we have laws regulating gambling (gambling, casino games, lotteries, etc.) which are circumvented by companies and those presenting their advertising outside the country. However, they have access to my approximate location and know that I am Swedish (using my language or currency in their advertising). They should be bound to refer at least to the helpline, see if they have access to information on my country. Dark patterns should be regulated or at least warned of. Many countries want minors to be protected from information trafficking, but this leads to adults being forced to link their legal identities to companies that intend to buy and sell their information. Therefore, all people of any age should be protected from information trafficking. Privacy should be prioritised over the income of info-giants. Attention has become an economy of its own, affecting the revenue of individuals and businesses. Influencers use knep as they say something inflammatory (do many arga), so they click on their posts, and can advertise more people, and gain more revenue. Often this is also a matter of policy. People can thus earn money from politically manipulating others. The ‘popular’ intervention is spread by the algorithm. Algorithms are to be addictive, especially with Endless scrolling on. Company “sells” products to a person that you do not even own. It may be a subscription service, temporary license, a product (often a game) that they do not intend to maintain or make your copy unusable after the company has gone on. More in society, mainly online, goes from ownership to renting from companies. That is wrong. You will be able to buy a good and own his copy and use it after the company ceases to invest in it. If you bought an audio book, you must own an audio book. If you bought a single-play game, you should be able to play offline years after the company went bankrupt. Laws in the UK and Australia have started to affect people online from Europe. For example, twitter/x tries to collect EU ID cards, something I believe is a security issue when the volatile platform is linked to a high-ranking man in a falling democracy with an unclear relationship with Russia as war against Ukraine, and is therefore considered by many as our country’s enemy. In addition, visa/Mastercard has been pressured by UK, Australia, and a small group of pro-censorship activists to steer what consumers all over the world pay for with their money. They have great power as two of the largest beta providers and press art/games platforms to change their rules. Many freelance artists and gamblers lose their income. Non-port-related games are censored, such as Mouthwashing, which takes a position against sexual violence against women. The game is feminist, therefore politically. In my view, bet.suppliers have used potential oligopolies for political influence. When states silence political messages, it violates freedom of expression. When they check what is published, it violates the freedom of the press. If companies at global level do these things, what should it be called? Businesses can do this because of a lack of alternatives. Proposal: take Japan. Create a cash payment system through shops or mail for online purchases. It reduces security problems as we become less susceptible to cyber-attacks that hit our digital economy.
